Case title: Methods-course experiment with predictive design tools and provenance logging
Fit to social science: Proposed design anchored in current evidence
Short description: Randomize doctoral or advanced methods students to standard design training versus predictive-AI-plus-provenance training to see which role distinctions are teachable.
Empirical unit of analysis: Student preregistrations, forecast logs, provenance records, rubric scores
Candidate roles tested: Counterfactual and transportability judge; calibration architect; protocol and provenance architect
Key typology question: Are calibration and counterfactual judgment distinct enough to teach and evaluate separately?
Observable artifacts / traces: Preregistration diffs, forecast logs, audit trails, grading rubrics, reflections
Key outcomes observed: Identification quality, calibration, over-trust, provenance completeness
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Students trained mainly in prompting perform just as well on identification and provenance tasks
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Artificial classroom settings may not map to real lab or dissertation pressures
Why this case has high leverage: This is the cleanest way to test whether the typology can structure training rather than only retrospective analysis.
Notes: Not yet directly studied in the specific configuration proposed.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
